literal: instrument of knowing; from prefix pra-, root √*mā* 'to measure, ascertain', and suffix -ana
invariant_definition: A *pramāṇa* is the operative means by which a cognition counts as *pramā* rather than *apramā*. The term belongs inside a larger epistemic fourfold: *pramātṛ* (knower), *pramāṇa* (means), *prameya* (object), and *pramiti* or *pramā* (resulting true cognition). It is therefore closer to an efficacious cognitive instrument than to 'evidence' in the modern sense. Disputes over *pramāṇa* are never merely about counting. Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Buddhist epistemology, and Vedānta disagree because they disagree about what kinds of objects there are, how cognition relates to them, whether absence-knowledge or postulation is reducible to inference, and whether testimony is autonomous. In Vedānta the decisive claim is that ordinary perception and inference govern *vyavahāra*, but *brahman* is disclosed only by *śabda-pramāṇa*, above all *śruti*. Hence debates about the autonomy of testimony, *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* versus *parataḥ-prāmāṇya*, and the exact relation between cognition and liberation are load-bearing parts of metaphysics, not scholastic appendices.

per_school.definition: Within *vyavahāra*, both *ātman* and *anātman* are established by *pratyakṣādi-pramāṇas*, but the latter is never given except as already resting on the self that illumines it [[NS 4.3](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.3)]. When the matter is *ātmatattva*, the Vedāntic *mahāvākyas* alone are *pramāpaka*, since the sentence-fit meanings of *tat* and *tvam* are not otherwise established and must be learned from *śāstra* itself [[SB Upodghāta](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/upodghāta)]. The Veda is therefore the decisive *pramāṇa* for liberating *jñāna*, and because its *phala* is the cessation of beginningless *mithyājñāna*, there is no *pramāṇa* for a *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)][[PV p. 164](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-vicāre kim-ānantaryam atha-śabdāvagamyam)]. Vivaraṇa Advaita further holds that *brahmakāra-śabda-pramāṇa* is in principle *aparokṣa*, though its effect is ordinarily obstructed until *mananādi-sahakāra* removes *viparyaya-saṃskāra* [[PV p. 103](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvākyasyāparokṣa-prayojakatvam)]. In the analysis of *bhrānti*, what appears has no *pramāṇa* for either *bhāva* or *abhāva*, and this failure of bivalent ascertainment underwrites *anirvacanīyatā* [[IṢ 1.9](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Anirvacanīya-khyāti-vādopasaṃhāraḥ)].

The *ātman* is *ananyasādhana* and self-manifest, so the economy of *pramāṇa* begins from a real knower irreducible to body, *indriya*, *manas*, *prāṇa*, or passing cognitions [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3)]. *Pratyakṣa* and *anumāna* are treated as genuinely world-disclosive: the *jagat* is *pratyakṣa* as *prameya*, and from its ordered dependence one establishes an *eka-pradhāna-puruṣa* who governs the ensemble of *cit* and *acit* [[ĪS 16-18](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/16-18)]. *Śruti* does not cancel what each domain's own *pramāṇa* establishes; rather, the *prapañca* is *siddha* by *svapramāṇa*, while *advitīyatva* excludes only a second that would be *sama* or *abhyadhika* to brahman [[Svs 31-35](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/31-35)].

Tattva-vāda treats the *sākṣin* as an *upajīvya-pramāṇa* that already apprehends *bheda*, and the *jīva-Īśvara-bheda* disclosed there is rendered *su-sthira* by *śruti* rather than annulled by it [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. *Abheda*-texts are accordingly read through *sādṛśya*, *prādhānya*, and *svātantrya*; they do not license *svarūpābheda* [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. What is *pramāṇadṛṣṭa* is, to that extent, *sat*; the *anirvacanīya* fails precisely because no *pramāṇa* establishes it, and whatever conflicts with *pramāṇa* functions as an *upādhi* [[p. 239, clause 11](cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 11)][[Anirvacanīye Pramāṇābhāvopapādanam](cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Pramāṇābhāvopapādanam)][[p. 239, clause 14](cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 14)]. Brahmaśāstra is thus the locus in which the *prāmāṇya* of the whole *śabdajāta* is settled, and later Tattva-vādins make that point explicit through a defence of *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* and of the Veda's being *pramāṇa* precisely as *apauruṣeya* [[BBsBh 1.1.1.15](cite://madhva/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.1.15)][[TT 1.5](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.5)][[TT Prāmāṇyavāda conclusion](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/conclusion)].

Acintya-Bhedābheda begins its system by asking after *pramāṇa* because the objects at issue are *alaukika* and *acintya*; owing to the *jīva*'s four defects, *pratyakṣa* and the rest are *sadoṣa* for that domain [[TS §9](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§9)][[TS §10](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§10)]. For inquiry into the all-transcending, all-supporting, inconceivable reality, Jīva therefore makes the Veda as *aprākṛta-vacana* the decisive *pramāṇa* [[TS §10](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§10)]. Within that *śabda*-domain, the *Sandarbha* proceeds by taking the *Bhāgavata* itself as the text to be examined for the determination of *parama-niḥśreyasa* [[TS §27](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§27)].

Śuddhādvaita insists that Brahman is not established anywhere in ordinary experience as either partless or partite; that issue is settled only because Brahman is *vedaika-samadhigamya* [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. The same *Aṇu-Bhāṣya* accordingly treats *brahmajñāna* as what renders the soul fit for *puruṣottama-prāpti*, so the decisive authority for Brahman is *śruti*-backed knowledge rather than any merely laukika route [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Sāṃkhya admits three *pramāṇas* only: *dṛṣṭa* or *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, and *āptavacana*, since *prameya-siddhi* depends on *pramāṇa* and no excess is needed beyond this triad [[SK 4](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4)][[SS 1.87-1.101](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.87-1.101)]. The commented *Kārikā* tradition explicitly absorbs *arthāpatti*, *sambhava*, *abhāva*, *upamāna*, *aitihya*, and the like into inference, thereby preserving a formally threefold economy of epistemic means [[SK 4 comm.](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4-commentary)].

In Yoga, *pramāṇa* is not a generic name for all valid cognition but one determinate class of *citta-vṛtti*, listed alongside *viparyaya*, *vikalpa*, *nidrā*, and *smṛti* [[YS 1.6-1.7](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.6-1.7)]. Its three members are *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, and *āgama*, and Vyāsa glosses them functionally as determinate object-contact, universal ascertainment through relation, and trustworthy verbal transmission from an *āpta* [[YBh 1.7](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.7)].

Nyāya canonically enumerates four *pramāṇas* — *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, *upamāna*, and *śabda* — and the opening sūtras define each as a distinct route to object-knowledge [[NS 1.1.3-7](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.3-7)]. Later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika handbooks preserve this fourfold structure both at the level of valid cognition and at the level of instrument, while distinguishing Vaidika *śabda* from merely human testimony by appeal to *āptavākya* and *īśvarokti* [[TSa 36](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/36)][[TSa 62](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/62)]. Classical Vaiśeṣika, however, still tends to fold *śābda* into *laiṅgika* and to articulate *āmnāya-prāmāṇya* within that leaner inferential economy [[VS 9.18-20, 10.19-21](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.18-20,10.19-21)].

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā grounds *pramāṇa*-theory in the claim that the word-meaning relation is *autpattika* and that, where the object is not otherwise available, that verbal connection itself is *pramāṇa* [[MS 1.1.5](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.5)]. In the specific domain of *dharma*, which is *śabdamūla*, the *aśabda* is not to be expected, and even a proposed *anumāna* is set aside when it conflicts with the *śāstric* basis of that domain [[MS 1.3.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1)][[MS 1.3.2-3](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.2-3)].

In the Mādhyamaka attack on *svabhāva*, *pramāṇas* cannot serve as self-grounding foundations for the establishment of things, because the very claim that *prameyas* are established by *pramāṇas* immediately raises the question of how the *pramāṇas* themselves are established [[VV 31-33](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/31-33)]. If they are established by further *pramāṇas*, *anavasthā* follows; if they are granted establishment without *pramāṇa*, the thesis that objects depend on *pramāṇa* collapses into *vaiṣamya* [[VV 31-33](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/31-33)].

The field of *pramāṇa* is twofold because the *meya* itself is twofold, distinguished by *śakti* and *aśakti* in *arthakriyā* [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.1](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.1)]. What is *arthakriyāsamartha* is *paramārthasat* and appears as *svalakṣaṇa*; what lacks that status is *saṃvṛtisat* and appears as *sāmānyalakṣaṇa* [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)]. The epistemic distinction therefore tracks the difference between what is directly efficacious and what is only conceptually fixed, so the *sāmānyalakṣaṇa* is not perceived as such and cannot sustain *anvaya-vyatireka* in the manner of a directly given particular [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.4](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.4)][[Pramāṇavārttika 2.5](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.5)].

Pratyabhijñā internalizes *pramāṇa* into the very self-manifestation of consciousness: in recollection, the self-awareness present there is itself the *pramāṇa* for the prior self-experience, not some external *liṅga* [[ĪPK 1.7.5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.7.5)]. More generally, Utpaladeva defines *pramāṇa* as the fresh self-appearance whereby a thing is determinately fixed, while *pramiti* is the unbaffled ascertainment that follows upon that reflexive manifestation [[ĪPK 2.3.1-2](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.3.1-2)]. At the highest level, however, the ever-luminous *pramātṛ* does not require a separate *pramāṇa* for its own *siddhi*; the need for *pramāṇa* belongs to the appearing novelties within consciousness, not to the abiding Lord-conscious subject itself [[ĪPK 2.3.16](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.3.16)].

Jainism pairs *pramāṇa* with *naya* from the outset: the *tattvas* are to be apprehended by *pramāṇas* and standpoints, so full cognition is at once many-sided and perspectivally articulated [[TAS 1.6](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.6)]. The *Tattvārtha-Sūtra* then classifies knowledge as fivefold and treats those five knowledges as *pramāṇas*, with *mati* and *śruta* as *parokṣa* and the remainder as *pratyakṣa* [[TAS 1.9-12](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-12)].

translator_note: Lazy glosses to avoid: 'proof', 'evidence', and 'source of knowledge'. 'Proof' imports a narrowly formal or mathematical model; 'evidence' imports a post-Lockean evidentialist psychology; 'source of knowledge' is too vague to show the *karaṇa* function. A *pramāṇa* is the causally efficacious means by which a cognition becomes *pramā* within a larger knower-means-object-result structure.
